title: Mizeria
subtitle: Polish cucumber and sour cream salad
excerpt: Thin slices of cucumber dressed with soured cream, dill, and a small amount of vinegar. The Polish table salad — made in ten minutes, eaten alongside everything from fried fish to breaded pork cutlets. Cool, clean, and properly sharp.
sourceNotes: Mizeria is a staple of the Polish table, documented in nineteenth-century Polish household manuals. The name derives from the Latin miseria — a reminder of its origins as a simple, inexpensive dish. The dressing is soured cream with dill and a small amount of acid; some regional versions use lemon, others cider vinegar.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Slice and salt the cucumber up to one hour ahead. Dress just before serving — the soured cream releases water quickly once combined.

content.text: Mizeria is the cold salad that appears on the Polish table regardless of what else is being served. It goes with kotlety schabowe, with fried fish, with roast pork, with almost everything. The simplicity is the point: sliced cucumber, soured cream, dill, a little acid, and salt. Made in ten minutes. Gone in five.

The one technique worth attending to is the salting step. Slicing the cucumber thinly and leaving it with a little salt for ten minutes draws out some moisture, which prevents the dressing from thinning out on the plate. Do not rinse the cucumber after salting — just press gently and drain. The residual salt seasons the dressing automatically.
A mandoline gives even, thin slices, but a sharp knife works well. You want the slices thin enough to be slightly translucent.

Slice {{cucumber}} cucumbers as thinly as possible. Place in a colander, toss with {{sea-salt-fine}}, and leave for 10 minutes. Press gently with your hands to release the excess liquid. Do not rinse.

Whisk together the {{soured-cream}}, {{cider-vinegar}}, {{granulated-sugar}}, and {{black-pepper-ground}}. Fold in the cucumber slices and most of the {{dill}}, reserving some for the top. Taste and add a little more vinegar if needed. The dressing should be sharp and cool. Serve immediately, scattered with the remaining dill.

items.cause: Cucumber not salted and drained, or salad dressed too early
items.fix: Salt and drain the cucumber before dressing. Dress just before serving and do not let it sit for more than 20 minutes
Taste after dressing and add more cider vinegar a teaspoon at a time, plus a pinch of salt. Mizeria should have a distinct tang

items.cause: Salad made too far in advance
items.fix: Add the dill only just before serving, or hold it back as a garnish

content.text: Mizeria appears in Polish cookery books from the nineteenth century and has not changed much since. The name, from the Latin for wretchedness, is a piece of irony that stuck: the salad was considered a fast-day or poverty food, made when nothing else was available. It is now eaten with the most substantial meals in the Polish repertoire, where its cool acidity cuts through breaded meats and rich stews. Some families use lemon in place of vinegar; others add a very small amount of sugar to round the dressing. The proportions are a family matter.
